preamble.summary: God finally speaks — not from a throne room, not from a burning bush, but from the whirlwind. After thirty-five chapters of human argument about the meaning of suffering, the Creator of the universe addresses Job directly, and the answer is not what anyone expected. God does not explain Job's suffering. God does not vindicate the friends' theology. God does not apologize. Instead, God asks questions — a relentless cascade of questions about the architecture of creation that Job cannot answer. Where were you when I laid the earth's foundation? Who set its measurements? Who shut the sea behind doors when it burst from the womb? Have you commanded the morning? Have you entered the storehouses of snow? Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades? The questions span the entire created order: earth, sea, light, darkness, weather, constellations, the laws of heaven and earth. The effect is not to humiliate Job but to reframe the conversation entirely — the question is not 'Why do I suffer?' but 'Do you understand the world you live in?'
preamble.remarkable: Job 38 is one of the supreme passages of the Hebrew Bible and of world literature. It is the only extended first-person speech of God in the Wisdom literature. The rhetorical strategy is extraordinary: God answers questions with questions. The Hebrew is at its most elevated and compressed — the poetry achieves effects that cannot be fully captured in any translation. The opening min ha-se'arah ('from the whirlwind/storm') is itself a theological statement: God speaks from within the chaos, not above it. The word se'arah carries connotations of storm, tempest, and violent wind — this is the same word used for Elijah's ascent (2 Kings 2:1, 11) and for theophanic storms throughout the prophets. God does not calm the storm before speaking; God speaks as the storm. The questions are not random — they follow a careful sequence from cosmogony (earth's foundation, the sea's birth) through meteorology (rain, snow, hail, lightning) to astronomy (Pleiades, Orion, the Bear). Each question implies the same answer: 'You were not there. You do not know. And yet the world holds together.' The theological force is not that Job is small and God is big — it is that the world is far more complex, more beautiful, more terrifying, and more carefully governed than any human theology can contain. The friends' neat system of retribution cannot account for the storehouses of hail or the channels of the thunderstorm. Job's demand for a legal hearing cannot account for the morning stars singing together. The speech does not answer Job's question; it dissolves the framework in which the question was asked.
preamble.friction: The central interpretive problem of God's speech is this: Is it an answer or an evasion? Job asked 'Why do I suffer?' and God responds with 'Where were you when I laid the earth's foundation?' Many readers — ancient and modern — have felt that God changes the subject, overwhelming Job with power rather than addressing his legitimate complaint. Others argue that the speech is profoundly responsive: it tells Job that the universe operates on principles far beyond retribution, that suffering and justice are embedded in a system so vast that no human mind can hold it all at once. The speech also creates a paradox: Job wanted God to appear (9:16, 13:22, 23:3-5), and now God has appeared — but not in the courtroom setting Job imagined. God comes as the Creator, not as a defendant or judge. The 'answer' is the encounter itself: Job asked to see God, and God has come. Whether that is enough depends on whether you think presence can substitute for explanation.
preamble.connections: The creation imagery in 38:4-7 parallels Genesis 1 but from a radically different angle — here creation is described not as a sequence of commands but as an architectural project with measurements, foundations, cornerstones, and a celebratory chorus of morning stars. The sea's birth in verses 8-11 personifies the ocean as an infant bursting from the womb, then swaddled in clouds — an image that connects to the Babylonian creation myth (Enuma Elish) where Marduk defeats Tiamat (the sea) but also to Psalm 104:6-9 where God sets boundaries for the waters. The constellations in verses 31-33 (Pleiades, Orion, the Bear/Mazzaroth) connect to Amos 5:8 and Job 9:9. The entire speech anticipates Psalm 104 and portions of Isaiah 40-45 where God's incomparability is demonstrated through creation. The phrase 'Where were you?' echoes God's question to Adam in Genesis 3:9 ('Where are you?') — in both cases, God's question reveals the creature's displacement from the center.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּ֤עַן יְהֹוָ֨ה ׀ אֶת־אִיּ֗וֹב מִ֥ן הַסְּעָרָ֗ה וַיֹּאמַֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Then the LORD answered Job out of the whirlwind, and said,
verses.rendering: Then YHWH answered Job from the whirlwind and said:
verses.expanded_rendering: The four-letter Name — YHWH — appears for the first time since the prose prologue. Throughout the dialogue, the speakers have used El, Eloah, Shaddai, but never YHWH. Now the covenant Name returns, and it returns in an act of speech: vayyaan YHWH ('and YHWH answered'). The verb anah ('answered') is stunning — it means God's speech is responsive. God is not launching an independent monologue; God is answering Job. And the answer comes min ha-se'arah ('from the whirlwind/storm'). The se'arah is not a gentle breeze or a still small voice — it is a tempest, a violent swirling storm. This is theophanic weather: Nahum 1:3 says YHWH's way is in the se'arah and the sufah. God does not quiet the storm to speak. God speaks as the storm. The whirlwind is both the medium and the message: the God who governs the uncontrollable forces of nature speaks from within those forces.
translator_notes: The return of the name YHWH is the single most important narrative signal in this verse. The friends and Job have debated about God using generic terms; now the personal, covenant Name speaks. This is not 'a god' responding — it is the God of Israel, the God who appeared to Moses, the God who brought Israel out of Egypt. The se'arah may connect to Elijah's experience in 1 Kings 19:11-12, where YHWH was not in the wind or earthquake but in the still small voice. Here, by contrast, YHWH speaks from within the storm itself. Different theophanies, different modes of divine speech.

key_terms.semantic_range: storm, tempest, whirlwind, violent wind, gale
key_terms.note: se'arah is the word for a violent, swirling storm — not ordinary wind but theophanic weather, the kind of atmospheric disruption that signals divine presence. It appears in 2 Kings 2:1, 11 for Elijah's ascent, in Isaiah 29:6 and 40:24 for divine judgment, and in Ezekiel 1:4 for the opening of the prophet's inaugural vision. In Job, the se'arah is the medium through which the Creator speaks — chaos itself becomes the vehicle of revelation.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֤י זֶ֨ה ׀ מַחְשִׁ֖יךְ עֵצָ֥ה בְמִלִּ֗ין בְּֽלִי־דָֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: Who is this that darkeneth counsel by words without knowledge?
verses.rendering:
Who is this who darkens counsel
with words empty of knowledge?
verses.expanded_rendering: God's first words to Job are a question — and the question is itself a challenge. Mi zeh machshikh etsah ('who is this who darkens counsel') — the verb machshikh ('makes dark, obscures') is from the same root as choshekh ('darkness'). Job's words have not illuminated the divine plan; they have obscured it. The word etsah ('counsel, plan, design') implies that there is a design, a purposeful arrangement to the universe, and Job's speeches have thrown shadows across it rather than clarifying it. The phrase bemillin beli da'at ('with words without knowledge') does not call Job a fool — it says his words, however eloquent, lack the knowledge needed to evaluate God's governance. The question is devastating but not dismissive: God does not say 'Be silent' but 'Who are you to speak about things you do not understand?'
translator_notes: The word etsah ('counsel, purpose, plan') is critical — it implies that the universe has a plan, a design that Job's suffering fits into, even though Job cannot see it. God does not reveal the plan but insists it exists. The phrase beli da'at ('without knowledge') will be answered by Job himself in 42:3 when he repeats this exact phrase and admits he spoke of things too wonderful for him.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱזׇר־נָ֣א כְגֶ֣בֶר חֲלָצֶ֑יךָ וְ֝אֶשְׁאָלְךָ֗ וְהוֹדִיעֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Gird up now thy loins like a man; for I will demand of thee, and answer thou me.
verses.rendering:
Brace yourself like a man.
I will question you, and you will answer me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The command ezar na khegever chalatsekha ('gird up your loins like a gever') uses the word gever — not adam ('human being') or ish ('man') but gever, the word for a warrior, a strong man. God is not mocking Job's weakness; God is honoring Job's courage by addressing him as a fighter. 'Stand up. Prepare yourself. This will be a contest.' The verb esh'alkha ('I will question you') reverses the entire dynamic: Job wanted to question God (13:22, 23:4-5); now God will question Job. And vehodi'eni ('and you will cause me to know, and you will inform me') is almost playful in its impossibility — Job is to teach God. The challenge is serious but not cruel: God enters the dialogue on Job's terms — question and answer — but reverses who asks and who answers.
translator_notes: The image of girding the loins refers to tucking the long robe into the belt to free the legs for action — running, fighting, or hard labor. It signals preparation for something strenuous. The use of gever rather than adam or ish is a mark of respect: God addresses Job as a warrior worthy of engagement, not as a worm to be crushed.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵיפֹ֣ה הָ֭יִיתָ בְּיָסְדִי־אָ֑רֶץ הַ֝גֵּ֗ד אִם־יָדַ֥עְתָּ בִינָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth? declare, if thou hast understanding.
verses.rendering:
Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth?
Tell me, if you possess understanding.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the question that defines the entire speech — perhaps the single most famous question in the Hebrew Bible. Eifoh hayita ('where were you') — the word eifoh ('where') demands a location. Job must account for his position in space and time during the founding of the world. The answer, of course, is: nowhere. Job did not exist. The phrase beyasdi arets ('when I founded the earth') uses the verb yasad ('to found, to lay a foundation'), treating creation as an architectural project — God as the master builder laying the groundwork of the world. The word yesod ('foundation') implies that the earth has a designed substructure, an engineered basis, not a random origin. The challenge hagged im yadata vinah ('declare, if you know understanding') is an invitation: if Job possesses the kind of understanding needed to evaluate God's governance, let him demonstrate it by answering these questions about creation.
translator_notes: The architectural metaphor — founding, measuring, laying cornerstones — runs through verses 4-7 and presents creation not as speaking things into existence (as in Genesis 1) but as constructing a building. This is a different creation tradition, one that emphasizes design, engineering, and craftsmanship. Both traditions affirm divine intentionality but from different angles.

key_terms.semantic_range: foundation, base, groundwork, basis, the bottom structural layer of a building
key_terms.note: yesod ('foundation') is an architectural term applied to the earth itself. The metaphor implies that the world has a designed substructure — it was not thrown together but engineered. The same word is used for the foundation of the Temple (1 Kings 5:17, 6:37, Ezra 3:10-11), connecting the creation of the world to the construction of God's house. If Job cannot explain how the earth's foundation was laid, he cannot evaluate the justice of the Builder's decisions.

verses.text_hebrew: מִי־שָׂ֣ם מְ֭מַדֶּיהָ כִּ֣י תֵדָ֑ע א֤וֹ מִֽי־נָטָ֖ה עָלֶ֣יהָ קָּֽו׃
verses.text_kjv: Who hath laid the measures thereof, if thou knowest? or who hath stretched the line upon it?
verses.rendering:
Who fixed its measurements — surely you know!
Or who stretched the measuring line across it?
verses.expanded_rendering: The questions intensify the architectural metaphor. Mi sam memaddeiha ('who set its dimensions') — the earth has dimensions, proportions, specifications. It was measured before it was made. The parenthetical ki teda ('for you know') is richly ironic — of course Job does not know, but the phrasing pretends he does, inviting him to supply the answer he cannot give. The parallel line asks mi natah aleiha qav ('who stretched the line upon it') — the qav is a builder's measuring cord, used to ensure straight lines and accurate proportions. God built the earth the way a master craftsman builds a structure: with plans, measurements, and precision tools.
translator_notes: The ironic ki teda ('surely you know') recurs as a rhetorical device throughout the speech. It does not mock Job so much as it emphasizes the gap between human knowledge and cosmic reality. The measuring line (qav) appears in Isaiah 34:11 and Zechariah 1:16 as an instrument of both destruction and rebuilding — God measures what God makes and what God unmakes.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־מָ֭ה אֲדָנֶ֣יהָ הׇטְבָּ֑עוּ א֥וֹ מִֽי־יָ֝רָ֗ה אֶ֣בֶן פִּנָּתָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Whereupon are the foundations thereof fastened? or who laid the corner stone thereof;
verses.rendering:
On what were its bases sunk?
Or who laid its cornerstone —
verses.expanded_rendering: The architectural metaphor reaches its climax. Al mah adaneiha hotba'u ('on what were its pedestals sunk') — the adanim ('bases, pedestals, sockets') are the foundation supports of a large structure, the same word used for the silver sockets of the Tabernacle (Exodus 26:19). The earth rests on supports that are sunk into — what? The question trails into mystery. There is nothing beneath the foundation of the earth; it rests on God's will alone. The parallel asks mi yarah even pinnatah ('who cast its cornerstone') — the even pinnah ('cornerstone') is the first and most important stone laid in construction, the stone that determines the alignment of the entire building. Someone laid the first stone of the earth. Who? The question answers itself: only God. But the asking of it reveals the scale of what Job presumes to judge.
translator_notes: The word hotba'u ('were sunk, were plunged') suggests the bases were driven deep, like pilings sunk into bedrock. The image implies massive engineering force. The cornerstone (even pinnah) appears in Isaiah 28:16 as a metaphor for what God establishes in Zion — the theological connection between the foundation of the earth and the foundation of God's kingdom is not accidental.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּרׇן־יַ֭חַד כּ֣וֹכְבֵי בֹ֑קֶר וַ֝יָּרִ֗יעוּ כׇּל־בְּנֵ֥י אֱלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: When the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy?
verses.rendering:
while the morning stars sang in chorus
and every divine being shouted with joy?
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse completes the creation scene with a moment of cosmic celebration. Beron yachad kokhevei voqer ('when the morning stars sang together') — the stars are not just astronomical objects; they are singers. They witnessed the laying of the earth's cornerstone and they sang. The verb ranan ('to sing, to cry out in joy') describes exuberant, ecstatic praise. The parallel line adds vayyari'u kol benei Elohim ('and all the sons of God shouted for joy') — the benei Elohim ('sons of God') are the heavenly beings, the divine council, the same figures who appeared in Job 1:6 and 2:1 when the Adversary came among them. At creation, they were not scheming or testing — they were shouting with joy. The picture is of a universe that began in celebration, not in conflict. The earth's foundation was laid to the sound of singing. This is perhaps the most beautiful verse in the book of Job and one of the most beautiful in all of Scripture.
translator_notes: The morning stars (kokhevei voqer) and the sons of God (benei Elohim) form a parallel pair, suggesting that the stars themselves are conceived as divine beings — or at least that heavenly beings and celestial objects belong to the same celebratory chorus. The image of creation accompanied by song appears nowhere in Genesis 1 but is developed in Psalm 19:1-4 ('The heavens declare the glory of God') and Psalm 148. This verse supplies what Genesis omits: the emotional atmosphere of creation. God made the world, and the universe applauded.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּ֣סֶךְ בִּדְלָתַ֣יִם יָ֑ם בְּ֝גִיח֗וֹ מֵרֶ֥חֶם יֵצֵֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: Or who shut up the sea with doors, when it brake forth, as if it had issued out of the womb?
verses.rendering:
Who shut the sea behind doors
when it burst forth from the womb?
verses.expanded_rendering: The metaphor shifts dramatically — from architecture to birth. The sea is not constructed; it is born. Vayyasekh bidlatayim yam ('and He shut the sea with double doors') — the sea needed to be contained at the moment of its birth, like a powerful infant that must be immediately restrained. The verb gasakh ('to shut, to hedge in, to block') is related to the word the Adversary used in 1:10 when he complained that God had hedged Job in protectively. Now God hedges in the sea. The phrase begicho merechem yetse ('when it burst forth from the womb going out') personifies the ocean as a baby emerging from the birth canal with explosive force. The womb (rechem) here is the womb of creation itself — the earth or the primordial deep. The sea is not evil, not a monster to be slain — it is a newborn to be swaddled and bounded.
translator_notes: The birth metaphor for the sea is unique to this passage and stands in contrast to the combat mythology of other ancient Near Eastern creation accounts where the sea-god must be defeated. Here the sea is not an enemy but a child — powerful, untamed, but ultimately subject to God's parental authority. The doors (delatayim) are a household image: God closes the nursery door on the raging infant ocean.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּשׂוּמִ֣י עָנָ֣ן לְבֻשׁ֑וֹ וַ֝עֲרָפֶ֗ל חֲתֻלָּתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: When I made the cloud the garment thereof, and thick darkness a swaddlingband for it,
verses.rendering:
When I made clouds its garment
and thick darkness its swaddling cloth?
verses.expanded_rendering: The birth metaphor continues with tender imagery. Besumi anan levusho ('when I set a cloud as its clothing') — God dressed the newborn sea in clouds, wrapping the ocean in mist the way a parent wraps a baby in cloth. The parallel va'arafel chatullato ('and deep darkness its swaddling band') extends the image: the arafel ('thick darkness, deep cloud cover') becomes the chatullah ('swaddling band'), the strips of cloth wound tightly around a newborn to keep its limbs secure. The infant sea is swaddled in darkness and cloud. This is one of the most surprising images in the Hebrew Bible — God as the tender parent of a wild, powerful ocean, wrapping it up, setting its limits not with violence but with care. The sea is both dangerous and beloved.
translator_notes: The word chatullah ('swaddling cloth') appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. It derives from chatal ('to wrap, to swaddle'). The image of swaddling connects to Ezekiel 16:4 where Jerusalem is described as an abandoned infant not swaddled at birth — here, by contrast, God swaddles the sea carefully. The theological implication is that God's relationship to the most chaotic force in nature is parental, not adversarial.

verses.text_hebrew: וָאֶשְׁבֹּ֣ר עָלָ֣יו חֻקִּ֑י וָ֝אָשִׂ֗ים בְּרִ֣יחַ וּדְלָתָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: And brake up for it my decreed place, and set bars and doors,
verses.rendering:
I broke upon it my limit,
and set bars and doors in place,
translator_notes: The verb va'eshbor ('and I broke/prescribed') alav chuqqi ('upon it my statute/limit') — God imposed a boundary on the sea, a decree it cannot cross. The chuq ('statute, decree, limit') is the same word used for divine law given to Israel. The same God who decrees moral law for humanity decrees physical law for the ocean. The bars (beriach) and doors (delatayim) complete the image: the sea is enclosed, contained, held.

verses.text_hebrew: וָאֹמַ֗ר עַד־פֹּ֣ה תָ֭בוֹא וְלֹ֣א תֹסִ֑יף וּפֹ֥א־יָ֝שִׁ֗ית בִּגְא֥וֹן גַּלֶּֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And said, Hitherto shalt thou come, but no further: and here shall thy proud waves be stayed?
verses.rendering:
and said, 'This far you may come, but no farther.
Here your proud waves must stop.'
verses.expanded_rendering: God quotes His own words to the sea — a speech within a speech. Ad poh tavo velo tosif ('to here you will come and not add/continue') — the boundary is absolute. The sea advances to a line drawn by God and cannot cross it. The phrase ufo yashit bige'on gallekha ('and here He sets a limit for the pride of your waves') uses the word ga'on ('pride, majesty, swelling') — the waves have pride, they surge and swell with force, but their pride breaks against God's decree. The theological point is precise: the most powerful, most relentless force in the physical world — the ocean — obeys a boundary set by God's word. If God governs the sea's pride, how much more does God govern the moral order that Job questions?
translator_notes: This verse is echoed in Psalm 104:9 ('You set a boundary they cannot cross; never again will they cover the earth') and Jeremiah 5:22 ('I placed the sand as a boundary for the sea, an eternal decree it cannot cross'). The proud waves (ga'on gallekha) use language elsewhere applied to human arrogance — the sea's physical pride and humanity's moral pride are governed by the same God.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲ֭מִיָּמֶיךָ צִוִּ֣יתָ בֹּ֑קֶר יִדַּ֖עְתָּה הַשַּׁ֣חַר מְקֹמֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Hast thou commanded the morning since thy days; and caused the dayspring to know his place;
verses.rendering:
Have you ever in your life commanded the morning?
Have you shown the dawn its place?
verses.expanded_rendering: The subject shifts from sea to sunrise. Hamimmeyamekha tsivvita boqer ('have you from your days commanded the morning') — the question is not just 'Can you make the sun rise?' but 'Have you ever, in the entire span of your existence, issued the command that makes morning happen?' The morning (boqer) comes every day, and every day it comes because God commands it. Job has never once been the cause of dawn. The parallel yidda'tah ha-shachar meqomo ('have you caused the dawn to know its place') personifies the dawn (shachar) as a servant who must be directed to its station. The shachar has a place, an assigned position, and every morning it goes there — not because of Job, not because of any human being, but because God shows it where to stand.
translator_notes: The dawn (shachar) is personified throughout the ancient Near East — in Ugaritic mythology, Shachar is a deity, the god of dawn. The Hebrew Bible demythologizes this: dawn is not a god but a servant, directed by God to its appointed place. The question implies that the daily miracle of sunrise is governed by the same intelligence that governs everything Job questions.

key_terms.semantic_range: dawn, daybreak, the first light of morning, the moment darkness gives way to light
key_terms.note: shachar ('dawn') is one of the key images in God's speech. In the ancient Near East, dawn was a liminal moment — the boundary between the realm of darkness and the realm of light. In Ugaritic mythology, Shachar was a deity. In the Hebrew Bible, shachar is a creature under God's command, sent daily to its assigned post. The theological force here is that even the transition from darkness to light — the most fundamental daily event — is something Job has never caused and cannot control.

verses.text_hebrew: לֶ֭אֱחֹז בְּכַנְפ֣וֹת הָאָ֑רֶץ וְיִנָּעֲר֖וּ רְשָׁעִ֣ים מִמֶּֽנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: That it might take hold of the ends of the earth, that the wicked might be shaken out of it?
verses.rendering:
so that it seizes the edges of the earth
and shakes the wicked out of it?
verses.expanded_rendering: The dawn is now described as a force of moral purification. Le'echoz bekhanfot ha'arets ('to seize the wings/edges of the earth') — the earth is imagined as a cloth or garment, and the dawn grasps its edges. Then veyinna'aru resha'im mimmennah ('and the wicked are shaken out of it') — as you shake crumbs from a tablecloth, the dawn shakes the wicked from the surface of the earth. Darkness is where wickedness hides; when light comes, the wicked are exposed and dislodged. The image links the physical phenomenon of sunrise to the moral structure of the universe: light reveals, exposes, and purifies. The dawn is not just beautiful; it is an instrument of justice.
translator_notes: The word kanfot ('wings, edges, corners') is the same word used for the corners of a garment (Numbers 15:38) and the wings of the Temple cherubim. The earth-as-garment image appears also in Isaiah 24:20. The shaking out of the wicked (na'ar, 'to shake off, to shake out') is a vivid domestic image applied to cosmic justice — God shakes the earth clean every morning.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּ֭תְהַפֵּךְ כְּחֹ֣מֶר חוֹתָ֑ם וְ֝יִתְיַצְּב֗וּ כְּמ֣וֹ לְבֽוּשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: It is turned as clay to the seal; and they stand as a garment.
verses.rendering:
The earth transforms like clay under a seal;
its features stand out like folds in a garment.
translator_notes: The imagery describes what dawn does to the landscape. Tithappekh kechomer chotam ('it turns like clay to a seal') — a seal pressed into clay reveals the image carved into it. When light comes, the formless darkness of night is pressed into shape, and the contours of the earth appear, as if a seal has stamped definition into what was blank. The parallel veyityatsevu kemo levush ('and they stand out like a garment') suggests the earth's features take on color and texture, like the patterns in a dyed fabric becoming visible in light.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיִמָּנַ֣ע מֵרְשָׁעִ֣ים אוֹרָ֑ם וּזְר֥וֹעַ רָ֝מָ֗ה תִּשָּׁבֵֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And from the wicked their light is withholden, and the high arm shall be broken.
verses.rendering:
From the wicked their light is withheld,
and the upraised arm is broken.
translator_notes: A paradox: the light that reveals the world also denies itself to the wicked. Veyimmana meResha'im oram ('and their light is withheld from the wicked') — the wicked operate by the 'light' of darkness; dawn destroys their working conditions. The uzero'a ramah tishaver ('and the raised arm is broken') — the arm lifted in violence is snapped by the dawn. The daily sunrise is a daily act of judgment against those who exploit the night.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲ֭בָאתָ עַד־נִבְכֵי־יָ֑ם וּבְחֵ֥קֶר תְּ֝ה֗וֹם הִתְהַלָּֽכְתָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Hast thou entered into the springs of the sea? or hast thou walked in the search of the depth?
verses.rendering:
Have you gone to the springs of the sea?
Have you walked the floor of the deep?
verses.expanded_rendering: The questions now plunge downward — from the sky to the bottom of the ocean. Hava'ta ad nivkhei yam ('have you come to the springs/sources of the sea') — the nivkhei are the subterranean springs that feed the ocean from below. Ancient cosmology understood the sea as supplied by underground sources connected to the primordial waters. Job has never visited them. The parallel uvecheqer tehom hithalakhta ('and in the searching/recesses of the deep have you walked') uses tehom — the cosmic deep, the primordial abyss, the same word that appears in Genesis 1:2 (tehom, 'the deep' over whose face darkness hovered). Have you walked the floor of the tehom? Have you explored the abyss? The answer is no. Job has never been to the bottom of existence.
translator_notes: The tehom ('deep, abyss') is one of the most resonant words in Hebrew cosmology. It appears in Genesis 1:2 as the primordial water over which God's spirit hovers, in Genesis 7:11 when the fountains of the great deep break open during the Flood, and in Psalm 104:6 as the waters that covered the earth before God set them boundaries. The tehom is the oldest thing in the universe besides God.

key_terms.semantic_range: deep, abyss, primordial waters, the cosmic ocean beneath and around the earth
key_terms.note: tehom ('the deep') is the primordial water-abyss that existed before creation took its ordered form. In Genesis 1:2, darkness was over the face of the tehom. In the Babylonian Enuma Elish, the cognate word Tiamat is the name of the primordial sea-goddess. The Hebrew Bible strips the word of its mythological personality but retains its cosmological weight: the tehom is the oldest, deepest reality in the created order, the boundary of human knowledge, the place only God has explored.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲנִגְל֣וּ לְ֭ךָ שַׁעֲרֵי־מָ֑וֶת וְשַׁעֲרֵ֖י צַלְמָ֣וֶת תִּרְאֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Have the gates of death been opened unto thee? or hast thou seen the doors of the shadow of death?
verses.rendering:
Have the gates of death been revealed to you?
Have you seen the gates of deep darkness?
translator_notes: The sha'arei mavet ('gates of death') and sha'arei tsalmaveth ('gates of deep shadow/death-shadow') describe the entrance to the underworld — Sheol, the realm of the dead. Job, who longed for death in chapter 3, has never actually been there. He romanticized death as a place of rest, but he has never seen its gates. God's question implies that death, like the sea and the dawn, is a domain God governs and Job has never visited. The parallelism between mavet ('death') and tsalmaveth ('deep shadow') echoes Job 3:5 where Job invoked tsalmaveth in his curse.

verses.text_hebrew: הִ֭תְבֹּנַנְתָּ עַד־רַחֲבֵי־אָ֑רֶץ הַ֝גֵּ֗ד אִם־יָדַ֥עְתָּ כֻלָּֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Hast thou perceived the breadth of the earth? declare if thou knowest it all.
verses.rendering:
Have you grasped the full breadth of the earth?
Tell me, if you know all of it.
translator_notes: The hitbonanta ('have you considered, have you perceived') ad rachavei arets ('unto the breadths of the earth') asks whether Job comprehends the sheer size of the world. The challenge im yadata khullah ('if you know all of it') is the most sweeping question yet: not a specific detail but totality. Do you know everything? The question is rhetorical — no one does — but it reframes Job's complaint: if you cannot even measure the earth, how can you measure the justice of the God who made it?

verses.text_hebrew: אֵי־זֶ֣ה הַ֭דֶּרֶךְ יִשְׁכׇּן־א֑וֹר וְ֝חֹ֗שֶׁךְ אֵי־זֶ֥ה מְקֹמֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Where is the way where light dwelleth? and as for darkness, where is the place thereof,
verses.rendering:
Where is the path to where light dwells?
And darkness — where is its place?
translator_notes: Light and darkness are treated as substances with fixed addresses. Ei zeh ha-derekh yishkon or ('where is the way where light dwells') — light has a home, a dwelling, a location it returns to when it is not illuminating the earth. Likewise darkness (choshekh) has a meqom ('place'). The cosmology is poetic rather than scientific, but the theological point is clear: light and darkness are not abstract concepts but created realities with assigned locations, governed by God.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֣י תִ֭קָּחֶנּוּ אֶל־גְּבוּל֑וֹ וְכִֽי־תָ֝בִ֗ין נְתִיב֥וֹת בֵּיתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: That thou shouldest take it to the bound thereof, and that thou shouldest know the paths to the house thereof?
verses.rendering:
Can you escort it to its territory?
Do you know the paths to its home?
translator_notes: The verbs are almost humorous: ki tiqqachennu el gevulo ('that you might take it to its border') imagines Job walking light home, escorting it to the boundary of its domain. Vekhi tavin netivot beito ('and that you might understand the paths to its house') — do you know the way to light's house? The personification treats light as a traveler who needs a guide, and the implication is that God is that guide. Every morning God escorts light out; every evening God walks it home.

verses.text_hebrew: יָ֭דַעְתָּ כִּי־אָ֣ז תִּוָּלֵ֑ד וּמִסְפַּ֖ר יָמֶ֣יךָ רַבִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Knowest thou it, because thou wast then born? or because the number of thy days is great?
verses.rendering:
Surely you know — for you were already born then,
and the number of your days is so great!
translator_notes: The sharpest irony in the speech so far. Yadata ki az tivvaled ('you know, for then you were born') — you must know how creation works because you were there when it happened. Umispar yamekha rabbim ('and the number of your days is great') — you have been alive so long that you must have witnessed it all. Of course Job was not born at creation. His days are not great on the cosmic scale. The irony is cutting but not cruel — it is the kind of challenge a teacher gives a student who has overstepped his knowledge.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲ֭בָאתָ אֶל־אֹצְר֣וֹת שָׁ֑לֶג וְאֹצְר֖וֹת בָּרָ֣ד תִּרְאֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Hast thou entered into the treasures of the snow? or hast thou seen the treasures of the hail,
verses.rendering:
Have you entered the storehouses of snow?
Have you seen the storehouses of hail,
translator_notes: The otserot ('storehouses, treasuries') of snow and hail — God keeps weather in storage, like grain in a warehouse. The image suggests that snow and hail are not random phenomena but stockpiled resources, stored until needed. The word otsar is used for royal treasuries and temple storehouses — weather is God's treasury, and God dispenses it according to purposes Job cannot see.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשֶׁר־חָשַׂ֥כְתִּי לְעֶת־צָ֑ר לְי֥וֹם קְ֝רָ֗ב וּמִלְחָמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Which I have reserved against the time of trouble, against the day of battle and war?
verses.rendering:
which I have stored for the time of trouble,
for the day of battle and war?
translator_notes: The hail is not just weather — it is weaponry. Asher chasakti le'et tsar ('which I have withheld for the time of distress') leyom qerav umilchamah ('for the day of battle and war'). God stockpiles hail as a military reserve. Joshua 10:11 records God hurling hailstones at Israel's enemies; Isaiah 28:2 and 17 describe hail as divine judgment. Weather is an instrument of providence, stored and deployed according to purposes that extend far beyond Job's comprehension.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵי־זֶ֣ה הַ֭דֶּרֶךְ יֵחָ֣לֶק א֑וֹר יָפֵ֖ץ קָדִ֣ים עֲלֵי־אָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: By what way is the light parted, which scattereth the east wind upon the earth?
verses.rendering:
By what path is lightning distributed?
How is the east wind scattered across the earth?
translator_notes: The ei zeh ha-derekh yechalek or ('where is the way light is divided/distributed') likely refers to lightning — the or here is the flash that splits across the sky in multiple directions. The parallel yafets qadim alei arets ('the east wind is scattered upon the earth') asks how wind patterns are generated. Both questions point to the same reality: weather has pathways, distribution networks, routing systems that Job has never mapped and cannot control.

verses.text_hebrew: מִֽי־פִלַּ֣ג לַשֶּׁ֣טֶף תְּעָלָ֑ה וְ֝דֶ֗רֶךְ לַחֲזִ֥יז קֹלֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Who hath divided a watercourse for the overflowing of waters, or a way for the lightning of thunder;
verses.rendering:
Who cut a channel for the torrents of rain,
or a path for the thunderbolt,
translator_notes: Mi fillag la-shhetef te'alah ('who split a channel for the flood') — the verb pilleg ('to split, to divide, to channel') describes the engineering of drainage and water flow. Even rainstorms follow channels. The derekh la-chaziz qolot ('a path for the flash of thunders') extends the image: lightning follows a path, a derekh, as if traveling a road God built. The questions continue to expose the hidden infrastructure of weather — systems Job cannot see or replicate.

verses.text_hebrew: לְ֭הַמְטִיר עַל־אֶ֣רֶץ לֹא־אִ֑ישׁ מִ֝דְבָּ֗ר לֹא־אָדָ֥ם בּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: To cause it to rain on the earth, where no man is; on the wilderness, wherein there is no man;
verses.rendering:
to bring rain on a land where no one lives,
on a wilderness with no human in it,
verses.expanded_rendering: This is one of the most theologically significant verses in the speech. Lehamtir al erets lo ish ('to rain upon a land of no man') midbar lo adam bo ('a wilderness with no human in it') — God sends rain to places where no person lives. Rain falls on the empty desert, on land no human eye sees, for no human benefit. This shatters the anthropocentric assumption that underlies both Job's complaint and the friends' theology: that the universe exists for human purposes, that every event must be explained in terms of human reward or punishment. God waters the uninhabited wilderness. The rain is not for anyone. It is for the land itself, or for God's own purposes, or simply because the desert exists and deserves water. The universe does not revolve around humanity.
translator_notes: This verse is a direct challenge to retribution theology, which assumes every divine act must serve human justice. God sends rain where no one can benefit — the rain serves purposes beyond human calculation. This is one of the clearest statements in the Hebrew Bible that God's providence extends beyond the human sphere and operates according to principles that include but transcend human welfare.

verses.text_hebrew: לְהַשְׂבִּ֣יעַ שֹׁ֭אָה וּמְשֹׁאָ֑ה וּ֝לְהַצְמִ֗יחַ מֹ֣צָא דֶֽשֶׁא׃
verses.text_kjv: To satisfy the desolate and waste ground; and to cause the bud of the tender herb to spring forth?
verses.rendering:
to satisfy the desolate wasteland
and make the grass spring up?
translator_notes: Lehasbia sho'ah umesho'ah ('to satisfy the desolation and waste') — the word sho'ah ('devastation, ruin, desolation') is used as a near-synonym with mesho'ah. God satisfies the waste — gives it what it needs, fills its emptiness with water. The ulehatsmiach motsa deshe ('and to cause the emergence of grass to sprout') — even in the desolate place, grass grows because God sends rain. The wilderness has needs and God meets them, regardless of human observation.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲיֵשׁ־לַמָּטָ֥ר אָ֑ב א֥וֹ מִי־ה֝וֹלִ֗יד אֶגְלֵי־טָֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: Hath the rain a father? or who hath begotten the drops of dew?
verses.rendering:
Does the rain have a father?
Who fathered the drops of dew?
translator_notes: Hayesh la-matar av ('does the rain have a father') — the question is deceptively simple. In Canaanite religion, the storm-god Baal was considered the father of rain. The Hebrew Bible's answer is implied: yes, rain has a father, and it is YHWH, not Baal. The mi holid eglei tal ('who fathered the drops of dew') extends the paternity metaphor. Rain and dew are God's children, born of divine will. The questions simultaneously demythologize pagan weather-gods and affirm YHWH's intimate relationship with meteorological phenomena.

verses.text_hebrew: מִבֶּ֣טֶן מִ֭י יָצָ֣א הַקָּ֑רַח וּכְפֹ֥ר שָׁ֝מַ֗יִם מִ֣י יְלָדֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Out of whose womb came the ice? and the hoary frost of heaven, who hath gendered it?
verses.rendering:
From whose womb did the ice come forth?
Who gave birth to the frost of heaven?
translator_notes: The birth metaphor recurs: mibetten mi yatsa ha-qerach ('from whose womb came the ice') — ice has a mother, a womb from which it emerged. The ukfor shamayim mi yelado ('and the frost of heaven, who bore it') asks who gave birth to frost. Like the sea in verse 8, frozen water is a child — born, not manufactured. The cumulative effect of these paternity and maternity questions is to reveal a creation that is intimately parented by God, not mechanically produced.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּ֭אֶבֶן מַ֣יִם יִתְחַבָּ֑אוּ וּפְנֵ֥י תְ֝ה֗וֹם יִתְלַכָּֽדוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The waters are hid as with a stone, and the face of the deep is frozen.
verses.rendering:
The waters harden like stone,
and the surface of the deep locks tight.
translator_notes: Ka'even mayim yitchabbe'u ('like stone the waters hide themselves') — when water freezes, it conceals itself inside stone-like ice. The pnei tehom yitlakkedu ('the face of the deep is seized/locked') describes ice forming over deep water — the surface of the tehom is captured, locked in place. The deep, which was born in verse 8 and explored in verse 16, is now imprisoned by its own frozen surface. The image conveys God's power over even the most primordial element of creation.

verses.text_hebrew: הַֽתְקַשֵּׁ֣ר מַעֲדַנּ֣וֹת כִּימָ֑ה אֽוֹ־מֹשְׁכ֖וֹת כְּסִ֣יל תְּפַתֵּֽחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: Canst thou bind the sweet influences of Pleiades, or loose the bands of Orion?
verses.rendering:
Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades?
Can you loosen the cords of Orion?
verses.expanded_rendering: The questions now soar from earth to the stars. Hateqasher ma'adannot Kimah ('can you bind the bonds of Kimah/Pleiades') — the Kimah is the Pleiades star cluster, a tight group of stars that appear bound together. The ma'adannot ('bonds, chains, delights') are what holds the cluster in its formation. Can Job tighten those chains? The parallel o moshkhot Kesil tefatteach ('or the cords of Kesil/Orion can you open') — Kesil is Orion, whose stars are spread across a wider area, as if straining against cords. Can Job loosen them? The questions assert that the constellations are held in place by forces only God controls — the architecture of the sky is as designed as the architecture of the earth.
translator_notes: Kimah (Pleiades) and Kesil (Orion) appear together also in Job 9:9 and Amos 5:8. The identification is generally accepted though not absolutely certain. The ma'adannot may also mean 'delights' or 'luxuries' — the Pleiades were associated with pleasant weather and spring rains. The moshkhot ('cords, drawing-bands') of Orion suggest the constellation's stars are held in tension like a bow or a yoke.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲתֹצִ֣יא מַזָּר֣וֹת בְּעִתּ֑וֹ וְ֝עַ֗יִשׁ עַל־בָּנֶ֥יהָ תַנְחֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Canst thou bring forth Mazzaroth in his season? or canst thou guide Arcturus with his sons?
verses.rendering:
Can you bring out the constellations in their season?
Can you guide the Great Bear with her cubs?
translator_notes: The Mazzarot are either the zodiacal constellations or a specific constellation group — the word appears only here and its identification is debated. The key point is be'itto ('in its season') — the constellations appear at appointed times, following a schedule Job did not set. The Ayish ('the Bear') with al baneiha ('upon/with her sons/cubs') is likely Ursa Major, the Great Bear, whose 'children' are the smaller stars trailing behind it. God guides the Bear across the sky as a mother leads her young. Even the stars are parented.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲ֭יָדַעְתָּ חֻקּ֣וֹת שָׁמָ֑יִם אִם־תָּשִׂ֖ים מִשְׁטָר֣וֹ בָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: Knowest thou the ordinances of heaven? canst thou set the dominion thereof in the earth?
verses.rendering:
Do you know the laws of heaven?
Can you establish its authority over the earth?
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is the hinge between astronomy and governance. Hayadata chuqqot shamayim ('do you know the statutes/laws of heaven') — the chuqqot are the same word used for Torah statutes, divine decrees, fixed laws. Heaven has laws — not just physical laws (gravity, orbital mechanics) but divine ordinances that govern how the sky relates to the earth. The im tasim mishtaro va'arets ('can you set its rule/authority in the earth') asks whether Job can enforce heaven's governance on earth. The word mishtar ('rule, dominion, authority') appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. The theological point is that heaven and earth are connected by laws Job neither made nor understands, and these laws govern everything — weather, seasons, tides, and by implication, the moral order Job questions.
translator_notes: The word chuqqot ('statutes, ordinances') applied to heaven connects the physical order to the moral order — the same God who legislates for Israel legislates for the cosmos. The unique word mishtar ('rule, governance') may be related to the Aramaic root for 'writing' or 'recording,' suggesting the laws of heaven are inscribed, permanent, authoritative.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲתָרִ֣ים לָעָ֣ב קוֹלֶ֑ךָ וְשִׁפְעַת־מַ֝֗יִם תְּכַסֶּֽךָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Canst thou lift up thy voice to the clouds, that abundance of waters may cover thee?
verses.rendering:
Can you raise your voice to the clouds
so that a flood of water covers you?
translator_notes: Hatarim la'av qolekha ('can you lift your voice to the cloud') — can Job shout at the sky and make it rain? The veshif'at mayim tekassekka ('and an abundance of water covers you') describes the result Job cannot produce: a downpour on command. Elijah prayed for rain (1 Kings 18:41-45), but even Elijah did not command it. God alone commands the clouds.

verses.text_hebrew: הַתְשַׁלַּ֣ח בְּרָקִ֣ים וְיֵלֵ֑כוּ וְיֹאמְר֖וּ לְךָ֣ הִנֵּֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Canst thou send lightnings, that they may go, and say unto thee, Here we are?
verses.rendering:
Can you send out lightning bolts so they go
and say to you, 'Here we are'?
translator_notes: Hateshallach beraqim veyelekhu ('can you send lightnings and they go') — the lightnings are messengers, dispatched on a mission. The veyomeru lekha hinnenu ('and they say to you, here we are') is extraordinary: the lightning bolts report back. They say hinneni ('here I am') — the same word Abraham used in response to God (Genesis 22:1), the same word Isaiah used (Isaiah 6:8). Lightning bolts are obedient servants of God. They go where sent and report for duty. The universe operates on a chain of command that flows from God, and Job is not in it.

verses.text_hebrew: מִי־שָׁ֣ת בַּ֭טֻּחוֹת חׇכְמָ֑ה א֤וֹ מִֽי־נָתַ֖ן לַשֶּׂ֣כְוִי בִינָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Who hath put wisdom in the inward parts? or who hath given understanding to the heart?
verses.rendering:
Who put wisdom in the hidden depths?
Or who gave the rooster understanding?
translator_notes: This verse is notoriously difficult. Mi shat ba-tuchot chokhmah ('who set wisdom in the tuchot') — the tuchot may mean 'inward parts' (the kidneys or inner organs as seats of wisdom) or 'ibis' (an Egyptian bird believed to predict the Nile's flooding). The parallel mi natan la-sekhvi vinah ('who gave understanding to the sekhvi') — sekhvi may mean 'mind/heart' or 'rooster' (which announces the dawn). Many modern scholars prefer the bird readings: the ibis and the rooster both demonstrate an innate wisdom — the ability to read weather signs — that was implanted by God. Whether the verse refers to human intuition or animal instinct, the point is the same: wisdom is God's gift, not humanity's invention.

verses.text_hebrew: מִֽי־יְסַפֵּ֣ר שְׁחָקִ֣ים בְּחׇכְמָ֑ה וְנִבְלֵ֥י שָׁ֝מַ֗יִם מִ֣י יַשְׁכִּֽיב׃
verses.text_kjv: Who can number the clouds in wisdom? or who can stay the bottles of heaven,
verses.rendering:
Who can count the clouds by wisdom?
Who can tip the water-skins of heaven,
translator_notes: Mi yesapper shechaqim bechokhmah ('who can count the clouds with wisdom') — the shechaqim ('thin clouds, sky, firmament') are innumerable, and counting them would require a wisdom beyond any human's capacity. The venivlei shamayim mi yashkiv ('and the skins of heaven, who can lay them down/tip them') — the nivlei are wineskins or water-skins. The sky is a collection of water-skins, and when God tips them, it rains. The domestic image — pouring water from a skin — applied to the cosmic sky is characteristically Joban: the grandest phenomena described through the simplest objects.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּצֶ֣קֶת עָ֭פָר לַמּוּצָ֑ק וּרְגָבִ֥ים יְדֻבָּֽקוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: When the dust groweth into hardness, and the clods cleave fast together?
verses.rendering:
when the dust hardens into a mass
and the clods stick together?
translator_notes: The betsheqet afar la-mutsaq ('when the dust pours into a casting/mass') describes what happens when rain hits parched ground — the loose dust fuses into hard earth. The urgavim yedubbaqun ('and the clods cling together') completes the image: scattered clumps of dry soil become a solid surface. This is a small, observational detail — mud forming — but it is one more thing God governs and Job does not. Even the behavior of wet dirt is part of the divine design.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲתָצ֣וּד לְלָבִ֣יא טָ֑רֶף וְחַיַּ֖ת כְּפִירִ֣ים תְּמַלֵּֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: Wilt thou hunt the prey for the lion? or fill the appetite of the young lions,
verses.rendering:
Can you hunt prey for the lioness?
Can you fill the appetite of her young,
translator_notes: The speech now shifts from cosmology and weather to animals — a transition that will continue through chapter 39. Hatatsud le-lavi taref ('can you hunt for the lioness prey') — the lavi is the lioness, the primary hunter in a lion pride. Can Job do her job? Can he provide food for wild predators? The vechayyat kefirim temalle ('and the life/appetite of young lions can you fill') asks whether Job can sustain lion cubs. God feeds predators. The theology is startling: God's care extends to carnivores, to the violent food chain that Job might consider unjust. God does not only feed the gentle and the good.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־יָשֹׁ֥חוּ בַמְּעוֹנ֑וֹת יֵשְׁב֖וּ בַסֻּכָּ֣ה לְמוֹ־אָֽרֶב׃
verses.text_kjv: When they couch in their dens, and abide in the covert to lie in wait?
verses.rendering:
when they crouch in their dens,
when they lie in ambush in the thicket?
translator_notes: Ki yashsochu ba-me'onot ('when they crouch in the dens') yeshvu ba-sukkah lemo arev ('they sit in the cover/booth for ambush') — the lions wait in hiding, and their hunt is successful because God designed them for it. The sukkah ('booth, shelter, cover') is the same word used for the temporary shelters of Sukkot — the lions have their own sukkah, their own shelter. The verse describes predatory behavior without moral judgment: lions ambush prey, and this is part of God's world.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֤י יָכִ֥ין לָעֹרֵ֗ב צֵ֫יד֥וֹ כִּֽי־יְ֭לָדָיו אֶל־אֵ֣ל יְשַׁוֵּ֑עוּ יִ֝תְע֗וּ לִבְלִי־אֹֽכֶל׃
verses.text_kjv: Who provideth for the raven his food? when his young ones cry unto God, they wander for lack of meat.
verses.rendering:
Who provides food for the raven
when its young cry out to God
and wander about for lack of food?
verses.expanded_rendering: The chapter ends not with the lion but with the raven — a bird considered unclean in Israelite law (Leviticus 11:15), a scavenger, a creature of waste places. Mi yakhin la'orev tseido ('who prepares for the raven its food') — God feeds the raven. The ki yeladav el El yeshavve'u ('for its young ones cry out to God') is remarkable: the raven's chicks cry to God, and God hears them. The scavenger's babies pray, in their way, and God answers. The yit'u livli okhel ('they wander for lack of food') describes the hungry wandering of raven chicks — and even this wandering is within God's care. The theological implication closes the chapter: God's providence encompasses creatures that human beings consider worthless, unclean, and peripheral. If God feeds the raven, the moral order is wider than any human system of merit and punishment.
translator_notes: The raven (orev) is the first bird Noah sends from the ark (Genesis 8:7), and it is listed as unclean in Leviticus 11:15. Yet God feeds it. Jesus echoes this verse in Luke 12:24: 'Consider the ravens: they neither sow nor reap... and yet God feeds them.' The crying of the raven chicks to God (el El yeshavve'u) attributes a kind of prayer to animals — they call out in need, and God responds. The universe is full of creatures in need, and God provides for all of them, not just the ones humans value.
